coredata.dc:title: Microgels based on 0D-3D carbon materials: Synthetic techniques, properties, applications, and challenges

Wi, Eunsol

coredata.dc:description: Microgels are three-dimensional (3D) colloidal hydrogel particles with outstanding features such as biocompatibility, good mechanical properties, tunable sizes from submicrometer to tens of nanometers, and large surface areas. Because of these unique qualities, microgels have been widely used in various applications. Carbon-based materials (CMs) with various dimensions (0–3D) have recently been investigated as promising candidates for the design and fabrication of microgels because of their large surface area, excellent conductivity, unique chemical stability, and low cost. Here, we provide a critical review of the specific characteristics of CMs that are being incorporated into microgels, as well as the state-of-the art applications of CM-microgels in pollutant adsorption and photodegradation, H2 evoluation, CO2 capture, soil conditioners, water retention, drug delivery, cell encapsulation, and tissue engineering. Advanced preparation techniques for CM-microgel systems are also summarized and discussed. Finally, challenges related to the low colloidal stability of CM-microgels and development strategies are examined. This review shows that CM-microgels have the potential to be widely used in various practical applications.

- Prepare single (W/O), double (W/O/W), and triple (W/O/W/O) emulsions

- Simple and highly robust

- Flexibility

- Gas permeability

- Preparing a large number of identical devices
- Low cost

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microgels are three-dimensional (3D) colloidal hydrogel particles with outstanding features such as biocompatibility, good mechanical properties, tunable sizes from submicrometer to tens of nanometers, and large surface areas. Because of these unique qualities, microgels have been widely used in various applications. Carbon-based materials (CMs) with various dimensions (0–3D) have recently been investigated as promising candidates for the design and fabrication of microgels because of their large surface area, excellent conductivity, unique chemical stability, and low cost. Here, we provide a critical review of the specific characteristics of CMs that are being incorporated into microgels, as well as the state-of-the art applications of CM-microgels in pollutant adsorption and photodegradation, H2 evoluation, CO2 capture, soil conditioners, water retention, drug delivery, cell encapsulation, and tissue engineering. Advanced preparation techniques for CM-microgel systems are also summarized and discussed. Finally, challenges related to the low colloidal stability of CM-microgels and development strategies are examined. This review shows that CM-microgels have the potential to be widely used in various practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Microgels are small 3D colloidal hydrogel particles that range from several micrometers down to the nanometer scale (Allazetta and Lutolf, 2015; Van Tran et al., 2018). Their physicochemical properties can be reversibly tuned by adjusting both their shape and volume in response to external stimuli (Reese et al., 2004; Kim et al., 2007; Oh et al., 2009; Guan and Zhang, 2011). Such unique features offer great potential for the utilization of microgels in various applications. Microgels can be fabricated from a large number of macromolecules, including polysaccharides, peptides, synthetic polymers, and biopolymers. Among these, polymers are often used for the preparation of sensitive or “smart” microgels, which have tunable swelling and shrinking behaviors, due to high hydrophilicity and structural flexibility (Tumarkin and Kumacheva, 2009). However, they possess several drawbacks such as a lack of sufficient stiffness, strength, and chemical functionality (functional chemical groups or anchoring groups). Thus, the incorporation of nanomaterials in the polymer network has been regarded as a potential approach for improving the mechanical, electrical, thermal, and physical properties of the fabricated microgels (Alam et al., 2018). CMs, including fullerenes, nano-onions, nanodots, nanodiamonds, nanohorns, nanotubes, and graphene-based materials have been widely investigated as ideal candidates for the design and development of microgels because of their unique properties (Eivazzadeh-Keihan et al., 2019). CMs exhibit a high surface area, excellent conductivity, and unique chemical stability. Moreover, there are abundant raw material sources (Georgakilas et al., 2015). In principle, composite microgels based on CMs will incorporate the advantages of CMs and microgels, which is expected to result in innovative microgels with excellent stability, large specific surface areas, and other desirable properties that can be adapted for various applications.

Hence, CM-microgels have attracted considerable attention in various fields. Each type of CM has its own strengths and limitations in relation to the synthetic process and applicability of the corresponding microgel. Therefore, selecting the appropriate CM for different applications is one of the most important strategies in designing a microgel with both high performance and cost effectiveness. The present review provides a comprehensive overview of potential CMs for the design and development of microgels, along with the state-of-the art applications of the CM-microgels. We summarize in detail the outstanding properties of several potential CMs and advanced techniques for the preparation of microgel systems. Moreover, promising applications of CM-based microgels in various fields (especially the environmental field and climate change issues) and their challenges are discussed. The main goal of this review is to provide promising approaches for the development and application of CM-based microgels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon materials for preparation of microgels
ce:section.ce:para: Microgels are commonly prepared by confining hydrogel solutions in microcompartments and curing them using physical or chemical techniques (Anjali et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2021). This section discusses the recent progress related to the role of some important CMs with various dimensions (0–3D) in the synthesis of microgels (Table 1 ).

0D carbon structures with quasi-spherical nanoparticles (<10 nm), known as carbon dots (CDs), include carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) (Xu et al., 2004; Sun et al., 2006; Zhu et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2017; Feng et al., 2021). CDs are incorporated in microgels for the fabrication of fluorescent microgels (Cai et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2017; Miao et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019). The solid matrix of a microgel control the interparticle distance, size, and shapes as well as enhance mechanical strength, and chemical and fluorescent stability of the CDs. In general, CDs can either be incorporated into the gel skeleton as adorned fluorescent moieties through electrostatic interactions, covalent bonds, noncovalent bonds, hydrogen bonds (Wolfbeis, 2015; Yan et al., 2021). For instance, CQDs containing surface functional groups (i.e., –COOH, –OH, and –NH2) were stably encapsulated into the polyethylene glycol (PEG) matrix for formation of a very green fluorescence microgel emission (Fig. 1 a) (Chandra and Singh, 2017). Novel photoluminescent microgels were also successfully fabricated by encapsulating CQDs in copolymer systems with two or three different polymer components (Wang et al., 2015; Campos et al., 2017). The diameters of these spherical microgels ranged from hundreds of nanometers to several hundreds of micrometers.

GQDs and GOQDs have also gained attention in the design and development of advanced fluorescent microgels due to their large surface area, local functional groups at the edges (edges effects), chemical inertness, good solubility, and shape and size controllability (Ritter and Lyding, 2009). GQDs and GOQDs with different surface functional groups such as ester (–COOR), amide (–CONHR), amine (–NHR), and hydroxyl (–OH) are often employed as the cross-linking agents with poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), chitosan (CS), carboxymethylcellulose (CMC), and other polymers (Baruah and Chowdhury, 2016; Javanbakht and Shaabani, 2019). Typically, such functional groups interact favorably with polymer chains through intermolecular hydrogen bonds. In particular, the functionalizing of GQDs with natural amino acids containing aromatic rings (i.e., histidine, phenylalanine, and tryptophan) often produces GQDs with improved electrochemical properties and surfaces that do not bend, twist, or fold (Ruiyi et al., 2017). Moreover, GQDs can be incorporated with noble metals such as gold (Au), palladium (Pd), and ruthenium (Ru) as an effective way to improve the electronic, chemical, and electrochemical properties of fluorescent microgels (Chen et al., 2016; Deming et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018a). For instance, the hybridizing of GQDs and Au–Pd nanowaxberries (Au–Pd NWs) (Fig. 1b) significantly enhances the electron transfer, electroactive surface area, and ion diffusion of the obtained microgel (Li et al., 2018a).

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are 1D nanocarbons (the two main types are single-walled nanotubes (SWNTs) and multi-walled nanotubes (MWNTs)) and possess an excellent combination of mechanical and electrical properties (Zhang et al., 2009; Bertels et al., 2014; Koh and Cheng, 2014; Gegenhuber et al., 2015; Rao et al., 2018; Wahid et al., 2020). Recently, the use of CNTs in a microgel matrix has attracted increasing attention for two reasons: (i) the addition of CNTs can improve the stiffness and induce conductive properties in microgels (Noël et al., 2015; Cui et al., 2016; Mottet et al., 2018; Ravanbakhsh et al., 2019), and (ii) the microgel also plays important roles as a dispersant and in decreasing the toxicity of the CNTs (Cui et al., 2016). Biocompatible polymers such as PVA, chitosan, gelatin, poly(N-isopro-pylacrylamide) (PNIPAAm), and sodium alginate are commonly incorporated with CNTs in microgels (Shi et al., 2005; Spizzirri et al., 2013; Mallakpour et al., 2021). The synthesis methods for CNT-based microgels are usually based on bottom-up approaches using the free-radical polymerization of monomers. For instance, a thermal/pH sensitive SWNT microgel based on a composite of chitosan and PNIPAAm was successfully prepared with for absorption and highly efficient conversion of near infrared (NIR) light to heat (Fig. 1c) (Qin et al., 2015). MWNTs were also used in a ternary composite with CDs and alginate to prepare a novel microgel bead with a superior adsorption capacity (Mallakpour et al., 2021). MWNTs were functionalized by carbonyl groups and then interacted with the alginate matrix via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction to obtain the microgel structure.

Graphene and its derivatives (i.e., graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxides (rGOs), and graphitic carbon nitride (g-CN), which is a well-known 2D nanostructure has been employed in the synthesis of hydrogels and microgels for various applications (Tahriri et al., 2019) owing to transparency, a high surface area, electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, and superior mechanical strength, (Lu et al., 2017; Choi et al., 2020). Because of the presence of aromatic rings, free π-π electrons, and reactive functional groups, the incorporation of these 2D CMs in a composite microgel not only improves its mechanical properties, but also considerably enhances other interesting properties such as the anti-oxidant activity, and effectively protects cells from high oxidative stress (Choe et al., 2019a; Choi et al., 2020). For example, monodisperse microgels comprising a PVA–GO complex were fabricated using osmotic-pressure-driven enrichment and hydrogen bonding interaction between GO sheets and PVA, where the GO sheets were aligned parallel to the water-oil interface (Fig. 1d) (Choi et al., 2020). Notably, the GO sheets served as a highly effective cross-linker in the matrix. Thus, the PVA–GO microgel still exhibited high stability and a uniform size. In another study, a novel microgel with the enhanced anti-oxidizing activity was obtained by combination of rGO and alginate (Choe et al., 2019a). While g-CN has been demonstrated to play various roles in microgel systems: (i) a stabilizer for monomer droplets (Xu and Antonietti, 2017; Cao et al., 2018), (ii) a cross-linker for connecting polymer chains during polymerization (Cao et al., 2019), and (iii) a reinforcing agent for improving the mechanical properties of the microgel (Zhu et al., 2014; Poostforooshan et al., 2016; Ko et al., 2017; Kumru et al., 2019). Therefore, the addition of g-CN to microgels is expected to initiate the polymerization process and fabricate recyclable materials. For example, g–CN–based microgels with a tailored size, swelling behavior, high photocatalytic properties, and recyclability were designed by using g-CN as a water-dispersible photoinitiator in combination with poly N,Ndimethylacrylamide (PDMA) (Cao et al., 2020a). Due to the presence of g-CN, these PDMA/g-CN microgels showed several outstanding properties, including facile size control in the range of 30 μm to 4 mm, with tailored swelling properties, distinctive stability, and simple recyclability.

To overcome the limitations of 2D carbon materials such as limited ion transport and reduced accessibility to the surface due to an intrinsic restacking tendency, 3D carbons networks with an interpenetrating microstructure have been considered to be promising candidates for the fabrication of microgels (Wang et al., 2021). Graphene networks with unique 3D morphologies have exhibited highly porous structures and excellent surface areas; they prevented the aggregation or restacking of individual graphene units (Wu et al., 2016; Olszowska et al., 2017; Zeng et al., 2021). Wu's group used 3D rGO and reduced graphene networks to synthesize a new microgel by a green and facile self-assembly process at low temperature and atmospheric pressure (Wu et al., 2016, 2020). As a result of improved interaction between the 3D graphene structures and analytes, this 3D graphene microgel showed a highly properties compared to 2D graphene microgels.

Nanodiamonds (NDs), a new 3D carbon nanoparticles with nanometer-sized carbon crystals (Lin et al., 2018), have been considered to be a potential candidate in the design of smart microgels for biological technologies and devices due to the very stable emission with a high quantum yield in the far red/near infrared region (Yu et al., 2005; Chang et al., 2008; Girard et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2018). For instance (Zhang et al., 2018), synthesized a novel poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (pNIPAM) microgel with embedded NDs in the core. In this study, ND with size ∼200 nm and the optimal thickness for the microgel shell was ∼100–200 nm. In another study (Zheng et al., 2019), used an ion gelation technique with detonation NDs and sodium alginate to produce a sunlight-responsive microgel (Fig. 1e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advanced techniques for preparation of carbon-based microgels
ce:section.ce:para: The major properties of microgels such as their size, structure, composition, stability, swelling behaviors, and configuration will determine their applications. Thus, it is important to synthesize microgels in a controlled manner with the desired properties. Some advanced techniques have recently been explored for the tailored fabrication of microgels (see Table 2 ).

Two dominant types of droplet MF devices, including glass capillary MFs and lithographically fabricated poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) devices, are widely used for generating various types of emulsion droplets in the synthesis of carbon-based microgels (Seo et al., 2015).

Generally, the use of a capillary MF device is considered to be a simple and highly robust fabrication process because it simply involves assembling basic modules consisting of injection, transition, and collection tubes (Chu et al., 2007). In capillary MFs, droplets are generated and manipulated at the microscale, where shear forces act between immiscible fluids (Shah et al., 2008; Tumarkin and Kumacheva, 2009). Thus, this approach is adequate for fabricating microgels with a spherical or sphere-like morphology (Seo et al., 2015). With the assistance of droplet capillary MFs, highly monodisperse microgels can be obtained using emulsion templating (a mixture of two immiscible liquids) through controlling the balance of various forces acting on the fluid flow (Zheng et al., 2021). Various types of emulsion templates including single water-in-oil (W/O) or oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions, water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W) double emulsions, and multiple emulsions t can be used to prepare carbon-based microgels.

The W/O droplet method is the simplest template, in which one fluid flows inside a cylindrical capillary and another fluid flows through the interstices between the square and the cylindrical capillary. Then they come into contact and form a 3D coaxial flow, producing a droplet (Fig. 2 a (i)) (Zheng et al., 2021). Using this template, microgels have a broad particle size range from the nano-to microscale (Rossow et al., 2012; Jans et al., 2017). Because of the domination of interfacial and viscous forces, the droplet formation dynamics can be modulated by the flow rates, viscosities, interfacial tension, densities, surface chemistry, and channel geometry (Nunes et al., 2013). For preparation of CM-based microgels, single W/O have been reported as an effective method (Chandra and Singh, 2017). For instance, a 3D microgel was prepared by an inner monomer stream consisting of PEG and CQDs, contacting with an outer oil phase (light liquid paraffin with 4% span 80). The results demonstrated that the microgels had small diameters (200–400 μm) and significantly larger surface area, compared to bulk gels. Moreover, size, architecture, and composition of microgels were easily tailored. Two different single-emulsions (i.e., O/W and W/O emulsions) were also used to fabricate a g-CN microgel system with monodispersity (Yandrapalli et al., 2020). The obtained microgel showed a narrow size distribution (45–116 μm) which can be modulated by the flow rate of the inner solution. The modified surface of g-CN prevented aggregation and enhanced the stability of the emulsions and microgels.

Glass capillary MF devices also offer a double emulsion method, by which one droplet is encapsulated inside another droplet, with higher controllability, thereby enabling the design and production of droplets with a core–shell structure (Van Tran et al., 2019). Combining the co-flow and flow focusing approaches is the most common method for designing MF devices for the fabrication of double emulsion droplets. Typically, the MF device contains two tapered cylindrical capillaries attached to opposite ends of a square capillary (Li et al., 2018b). For the droplet generation mechanism in a double emulsion system, the inner phase fluid is channeled through the tapered injection cylindrical capillary, and the middle phase fluid is guided through the interstices between the square capillary and the injection cylindrical capillary in the same direction (Fig. 2a (ii)). In the opposite direction, the outermost fluid is channeled through the interstices between the collection cylindrical capillary and the square capillary. The double emulsion droplets are obtained instantly when the three immiscible liquids flow into a collecting tube. Currently, double emulsion templates are widely used to fabricate carbon-based microgels (Byun et al., 2015; Choi et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2021). (Choi et al., 2020) presented a double emulsion template based on a capillary MF device to prepare monodisperse PVA–GO and poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO)-GO microgels using an osmotic-pressure-driven enrichment approach. In this research, PVA and GO were dissolved in the cores of double-emulsion droplets, and PVA-GO microgels were formed by the shrinkage and enrichment of the core and the sol–gel transition through the hydrogen bonding of PVA-GO. The resulting PVA-GO microgels possessed high monodispersity, with an average radius of 45.4 μm. In the latest research (Lee et al., 2021), also introduced spherical microdroplets with highly uniform diameters (220–330 mm) to GO nanosheets and PVA using a W/O/W MF system (Fig. 2b). Moreover, based on the double emulsion droplet mechanism, strong confinement effect and stability of the GO in the microgel structure could be derived by adjusting the GO concentration, microdroplet size, and GO nanosheet size.

Higher-order multiple emulsions (triple-, quadruple-) can be developed based on capillary MF devices by adding additional co-flow junctions composed of injection and transition tubes (Chu et al., 2007; Wang et al., 2011). In these systems, the droplet stability and controllability can be improved by treating the surfaces of the glass capillaries. The high stability makes it possible to accurately control the droplet size and number of inner droplets in multiple emulsions. Generally, triple-emulsion droplets can be formed in a single step without sequential emulsification (Seiffert, 2013). For instance, triple monodisperse emulsion droplets with an ultrathin water layer were successfully fabricated using a simple microfluidic emulsification, where a W/O biphasic flow was injected into a capillary co-flow with an oil phase and emulsified by an additional continuous aqueous phase (Fig. 2c) (Choi et al., 2016). This triple emulsion could form an ultrathin water layer by the addition of a microgel precursor, which separated the polymerized microgel shell from the hydrophobic core. The resulting microgels showed high monodispersity, with diameters of 90–95 μm.

Although capillary MF devices have some advantages for the fabrication of microgels, such as a simple design, high monodispersity and unity, and ability to control the size and morphology, they also have several disadvantages including a manual fabrication process, difficulty producing large numbers of devices, and lack of reproducibility (Chu et al., 2007; Shah et al., 2008; Tumarkin and Kumacheva, 2009). Therefore, PDMS MF devices have been widely developed as a promising alternative because of the simplicity and reproducibility of the fabrication procedure (Seiffert and Weitz, 2010). Compared to glass capillary MFs, PDMS devices are often fabricated using soft lithography, which can produce numerous identical devices with higher flexibility and a smaller channel scale based on replica molding. These devices for microgel generation are commonly designed based on a flow-focusing geometry because of the simplicity of controlling microgel diameters through tuning the gel and oil flow rates, which results in small monodisperse droplets (Jiang et al., 2016a). The flow-focusing geometry of a PDMS MF device principally uses the shear stress of the oil phase to generate aqueous droplets. Based on this geometry (Cha et al., 2014), produced highly uniform droplets, which resulted in the formation of microgels with microscale diameters in the range 35–150 μm (Fig. 3 a). The size could be tailored by varying the shear stress through the applied aqueous flow and the ratio of the flow rates of the aqueous and oil flows.

In the industry, the use of a single PDMS nozzle device for the fabrication of microgels has been significantly limited because of its low production rate and low volumetric throughput (Mottin et al., 2021). To overcome this limitation, many recent studies have assembled multiple parallel channel systems to increase the droplet production rate (Vladisavljević et al., 2013; Amstad et al., 2016). When parallelizing multiple flow-focusing PDMS MF devices, all the flow-focusing junctions were connected to two separate general inlets, whereas all the formed droplet flows were collected in one general outlet (Femmer et al., 2015). However, the coupled inlet and outlet flow paths of a PDMS device exhibited significant challenges related to the controllability and maintenance of small and uniform droplet diameters because of the propagation of pressure fluctuations between nozzles. Recently, parallel PDMS MFs have been developed for the production of microgels using light-initiated free-radical polymerization on layer MFs, which can produce uniform, robust, and massive double emulsions (Romanowsky et al., 2012; Kim et al., 2013; Amstad et al., 2016). For instance (Headen et al., 2018), successfully developed a novel process for the high-throughput production of sub-100 μm diameter microgels using a novel two-layer MF device with six parallel flow-focusing nozzles (Fig. 3b). This device comprised two nozzles with different widths: 35 μm wide nozzles could generate smaller microgels by breaking up droplets from 100 μm wide nozzles. With six parallel channels, this MF device reduced the total production time and increased the volumetric throughput six-fold compared to a single-nozzle device.

After the droplets are generated in the MF device, microgels are obtained by a crosslinking process. Two methods are used for the crosslinking gelation of droplets: on-chip and off-chip. Off-chip MF devices offer prolonged gelation (Zhang et al., 2020c), whereas on-chip MF devices provide a better approach for high productivity, ease of handling, and cell viability (Lin et al., 2021). The chemical crosslinking method is the most common approach for producing microgels in an on-chip MF device (Jiang et al., 2016a). Moreover, on-chip photo-crosslinking under high intensity UV irradiation within a short time (10 s) is another promising benefit provided by the development of MF chips for the fabrication of microgels (Nie et al., 2020). Recently, stable, continuous, and integrated MF chips have been developed for the high-throughput fabrication of microgel droplets with low immunogenicity and high non-toxicity (Nichol et al., 2010; Yue et al., 2015). These MF chips considerably reduced the loss of the formed droplets throughout the subsequent steps of microgel droplet generation by combining all the production steps, including droplet generation, photo-crosslinking, and oil filtration and washing, within one chip (Fig. 3c) (Mohamed et al., 2019). The integration of several stages enables MF chips to deliver a high yield of the produced microgels by eliminating any off-chip requirements for handling the droplets. Moreover, the traditional techniques for separating the microgel from the oil (i.e., centrifugation and washing with buffer solutions) often used a high speed and longer centrifugation time (Deng et al., 2011; Hong et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2017). MF chips can resolve this problem by translocating microgels from the oil into an aqueous phase through a hydrodynamic filtration method, where a sacrificial layer of oil is immediately de-wetted from the microgel surface when it comes into contact with the aqueous phase buffer (Mohamed et al., 2019).

For the rapid synthesis and screening of thousands of microgels with distinct compositions, programmable droplet MF chips have been recently discovered as a potential technique (Allazetta et al., 2017). fabricated an unprecedented diversity of microgel formulations via a programmable droplet MF chip mixing different reactive molecular hydrogel building blocks from a library. In this approach, droplet compositions that included macromers and bioactives could be systematically modulated to generate a population of microgels by programming the flow rates of different inlets (Allazetta et al., 2017). This approach used conventional fluorescence microscopy or flow cytometry to rapidly screen and discover specific microgel formulations (Chiang et al., 2016). also developed an electromicrofluidic chip that followed predesigned programs without shape, material, and programmability constraints. This MF chip had various reservoirs and was programmed to aliquot, mix, transport, and position various crosslinkable solutions, dispersed particles, and soluble molecules in the microgel droplet synthesis process. Furthermore, the microgels could be encoded spectrally with adjustable colors, geometrically with different shapes, or graphically with various embedded particles. Therefore, the electromicrofluidic chip could provide versatile electric manipulations for the fabrication of microgels with reorganized particles and heterogeneous architectures.

Electrospraying is a novel method for producing micro- and nanogels based on the ability of an electric field to deform the interface of droplets and the crosslinking or photo-crosslinking reactions between polymers and CMs in the aqueous solution (Fig. 4 a) (Xin et al., 2019). The theory of charged droplets has been assumed to be a major factor in the formation of microgels in an electrospray system. Specifically, the applied electric charge causes an electrostatic force (or Coulomb force) that competes with the cohesion force in the droplets (Tapia-Hernández et al., 2015; Tran and Lee, 2018). Nano- or microdroplets are formed through the release of the surface tension when the Coulomb force is greater than the cohesive force. In addition, electrospraying can generate microgel aerosols through the electrostatic dispersion of aqueous solutions, which makes it possible to produce highly monodisperse microgels, enhance their clinical effect, and reduce their toxicity (Lai et al., 2017). In this approach, parallel solution streams are combined in a jet to form droplets (Fig. 4b) (Lai et al., 2016). Without an electric field, the interplay between the surface tension and gravity is responsible for the formation of these droplets, whereas the electrostatic force will increase when an electric field is applied, stretching the liquid flow dispensed through the nozzle and forming a tapered jet. An increase in the electric field results in the dominance of the electrostatic force in the pulling force against the surface tension instead of the gravitational force; this phenomenon generates more monodisperse droplets. In electrospray technology, the microgel size can be simply tuned by controlling the flow rate and electric field strength. In general, a higher electric field can produce smaller microgels.

Porous microgels can be fabricated using two sub-types of electrospray technologies: microemulsion-assisted electrospray (MEAES) (Maeng et al., 2010; Malik et al., 2016) and nonsolvent-assisted electrospray (NSAES) (Gao et al., 2014, 2016). In the MEAES technique, micro/nano droplets containing double emulsion droplets of porogens are first synthesized by electrospraying a bulk microemulsion solution. Porous microgels are then obtained by etching or removing the porogens (Malik et al., 2016). In the NSAES process, porous microgels are formed by the nonsolvent-induced phase separation of a polymer mixture (polymers are dispersed in an insoluble solvent), following a curing step (Gao et al., 2014). Microgel pores are formed in the polymer-lean phases. These results also provide a promising approach for controlling the porosity of microgels using electrospray technology, with greatly improved permeability and higher specific surface areas compared to smooth microgels.

Their softness and high responsiveness to various external stimuli such as temperature, pH, and other analytes make carbon-based microgels potential soft model systems in a wide range of applications. Moreover, their excellent adsorption capacity, colloidal stability, and large specific surface area make them some of the most important candidates for environmental, biological, and biomedical applications such as pollutant removal, sensors, and drug delivery.

Like other conventional hydrogels, microgels also possess an excellent adsorption capacity for impurities in water as a result of their 3D structures. Moreover, graphene and GO-based materials are well-known as promising adsorbents for the effective removal of impurities in aqueous solutions because of their outstanding features related to the large surface areas of the (quasi-)monolayers, numerous polar surface groups, and edges (Fu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017b). However, 2D sheets of graphene and its derivatives can aggregate as a result of strong interplanar interactions, which results in a decreased adsorption capacity. Moreover, it is difficult to collect GO powder from water for reuse and recycling (Chen et al., 2013). Therefore, GO-based microgels have been widely applied for the adsorption of both heavy metals and organic dyes because they can solve the aforementioned problems (Sun et al., 2018; Pan et al., 2019; Choi et al., 2020). It has been demonstrated that GO makes significant contributions to the adsorption capacities of different microgels in the organic dye removal process (Choi et al., 2020). For instance, the adsorption capacities of PVA-GO microgels for various dyes such as crystal violet (CV), methylene blue (MB), and malachite green (MG) increase with the GO concentration in an approximately linear relation. This increased capacity is ascribed to the high density of negatively charged functional groups on the GO sheets. In another study (Sun et al., 2018), prepared a PEG-GO microgel with hierarchical porous structures and controllable movement capability by incorporating hollow cores and encapsulating magnetic nanoparticles into the double micro-emulsion droplets. These features further improved the adsorbing efficiency of the PEG-GO microgels for MB and Nile red (NR). In particular, these PEG-GO microgels acted as an excellent adsorbent for polar organic impurities in oil (Fig. 5 a). The adsorption ability of PEG-GO microgels for lipophilic dyes can be explained by the large number of polar groups on the surfaces and edges of the GO, which can facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds between the microgels and dyes. Compared to PEG microgels, PEG-GO composite microgels showed a superior adsorption efficiency for both MB and NR, which was attributed to the direct interaction between the organic dye molecules and functional groups of the GO layers. Interestingly, porous PEG-GO microgels exhibited twice the specific surface areas and adsorption probabilities, which were beneficial for impurity adsorption. Porous PEG-GO microgels are generated using hollow cores in multiple droplets (Sun et al., 2018). Moreover, it is expected that the incorporation of GO in microgels would make it possible to use them in dye degradation and recycling adsorption processes because of the high photocatalytic activity of GO (Gunture et al., 2019)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and detection of heavy metals
ce:section.ce:para: GO layers possess numerous O atoms in the form of epoxy, –OH, and –COOH groups on the basal plane and edge of the sheets, which can bind heavy metal ions through coordination and electrostatic interaction (Park et al., 2008; Dreyer et al., 2010; Xu et al., 2010). Therefore, GO microgels have also been designed for heavy metal adsorption. The main adsorption mechanisms of GO microgels are the synergistic effects of electrostatic interactions, surface complexation, and ion exchange. For instance, a carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)-GO microgel with 2.2–3.6 μm diameters was effectively used as a potential adsorbent for various heavy metal ions, including Pb(II), Cd(II), and Cu(II) (Liu et al., 2016). The presence of GO increased the adsorption capacity and reaction time of the microgel for heavy metal ions. Similar to dye adsorption, the metal adsorption efficiency of GO-microgels can be controlled by varying the GO content and solution pH. To endow a convenient magnetic separability, GO microgels have recently been developed for heavy metal adsorption by decorating superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles onto the GO layers (Deng et al., 2013; Cui et al., 2015; Sherlala et al., 2018). Moreover, the fabrication of high-performance microgels for the recognition and removal of specific metal ions from aqueous solutions is highly desired. Hence, magnetic GOs have been used to design smart microgels with excellent magnetism and highly sensitive ion-recognition selectivity. For example (Pan et al., 2019), introduced a smart microgel composed of magnetic GO and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide-co-benzo-18-crown-6 acrylamide) (PNB) by simultaneously immobilizing Fe3O4 nanoparticles and PNB molecules onto GO nanosheets (Fig. 5b). In this microgel, the PNB served as a host, selectively recognizing and absorbing Pb(II) ions, while the loaded Fe3O4 particles facilitated the separability of the microgel from the aqueous solution. Furthermore, the incorporation of poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) functional chains on the GO produced microgels capable of excellent thermosensitive adsorption of Pb(II). Most importantly, such multifunctional GO microgels could easily be regenerated and reused many times. Such results demonstrated that graphene and GO-microgels are promising adsorbents for the adsorption and removal of specific heavy metals from an aqueous solution (Pan et al., 2019)

CDs, which are novel fluorescent carbon materials, have recently attracted considerable attention in relation to the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions because of their interesting optical features and the various functional groups on their surfaces (Guo et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020a). Generally, CDs made from plants or other natural sources are expected to greatly improve the adsorption capacities of microgels for heavy metal ions because they consist of phytoconstituents with heteroatoms (Naik et al., 2020). Moreover, these CDs are enriched with nitrogen atoms (Atchudan et al., 2018; Raji et al., 2019), which can also improve the heavy metal ion adsorption of microgels. Therefore, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesized from natural sources are widely utilized for the preparation of microgels for the detection and separation of heavy metal ions (Gogoi et al., 2015; Ehtesabi et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020a). (Perumal et al., 2022) used N-CDs based on red Malus floribunda fruits to prepare chitosan microgels for the adsorption of Pb(II), Cd(II), Cr(III), and Hg(II). This study confirmed that the introduction of N-CDs into a microgel matrix greatly improved the adsorption efficiency, compared to other hydrogels or microgels without N-CDs. N-CDs consist of nitrogen atoms in the form of amine functional groups, which can enhance the adsorption capacity of a microgel by facilitating electrostatic interactions between the microgel and heavy metal ions. These N-CD microgels exhibited an excellent adsorption efficiency (92–99%) for Hg(II) ions, as well as the ability to simultaneously adsorb multiple metal ions, which could be beneficial in practical applications. To take advantage of the high-performance luminescence of CDs in combination with the high adsorption of biopolymer hydrogels, several fluorescent microgels have been designed and used, not only as potential adsorbents, but also as optical sensors for the detection of heavy metals (Guo et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020b; Yuan et al., 2021). The results of these studies showed that the 3D porous structures of microgels provided a large number of active sites and stabilized the CDs, thus enhancing the adsorption efficiency. For example, the specific mechanism of CD-cellulose microgels responsible for the high adsorption capacity and detection efficiency for Cr(VI) is proposed as Fig. 5c (Yuan et al., 2021). The microgels could quickly absorb Cr(VI) ions on their surfaces as a result of the electrostatic attraction between the amino groups originating from the CDs and metal ions. The 3D porous structures further increased the surface areas of the microgels, and the adsorption sites could easily contact the Cr(VI) ions, resulting in an excellent Cr(VI) adsorption ability. In addition, the CDs embedded in the 3D porous microgels enabled their use as a probe for sensing Cr(VI) ions. The metal ions were detected through a rapid fluorescence quenching effect caused by their adsorption on the CD surface. Such results show that CD-based microgels are promising candidates for applications in environment fields, especially in the detection and removal of heavy metal ions.

Carbon capture and storage technologies are among the most promising strategies for reducing atmospheric CO2 concentration and preventing global warming (Hsan et al., 2022). In general, materials with a porous structure can be considered as potential absorbents with favorable CO2 capture capacity due to excellent physical adsorption. Microgels have been recently offered as good absorbents for CO2 capture (Yue et al., 2014; Yao et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020). Furthermore, the affinity between microgels and CO2 may be controlled by incorporating amine groups and/or by modulating the microenvironment around the amine groups in the microgels (Fig. 5d) (Hoshino et al., 2021). In particular, GO has attracted huge attention for CO2 adsorption and conversion (Sui et al., 2013). Nonetheless, the strong stacking of GO sheets highly limits its gas adsorption capacity. Thus, the functionalization of GO with nitrogen-containing compounds, and its incorporation in the microgel matrix, will provide an excellent strategy for increasing its CO2 capture and conversion capacity (Huang and Feng, 2018; Hsan et al., 2019). (Hsan et al., 2022) developed a novel microgel adsorbent based on an N-functionalized GO and arginine for CO2 capture with catalytic activity. The specific surface area and CO2 gas capture capacity of the microgel were 22.07 m2/g and 24.15 wt% (5.48 mmol/g), respectively. The GO dispersion into the chitosan matrix resulted in a highly increased surface area, thereby inducing the subsequent increase in CO2 sorption (Alhwaige et al., 2013). Similarly, rGO and CNTs have been used for the design of microgels for CO2 capture applications (Li et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2017; Xia et al., 2020). (Xia et al., 2020) prepared an rGO microgel that can provide a highly multifunctional structure and porous support for adsorptive desulfurisation and high-pressure CO2 capture. The microgel sorbents exhibited excellent total CO2 sorption capacities (2.36 mmol/g). Meanwhile Zheng et al. used CNTs to prepare a series of composite microgels incorporating polyetheramine in the shell for CO2 gas adsorption. The CO2 uptake performance of the microgels was influenced by the components and content of polyetheramine in the shell, as well as the size, structure, and component of the core.

Adsorption is a conventional technology with limitations for the removal of organic pollutants from water. Therefore, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), especially photocatalysis, have attracted attention as advanced wastewater treatment technologies. Microgels have been regarded as a potential templates and platforms for the development of advanced photocatalysts for AOPs of pollutants (Li et al., 2022). Microgels can quickly adsorb and enrich pollutants, stabilize catalysts, and provide multiple catalytic sites for redox reactions. In particular, g-CN has emerged as an active visible-light-driven photocatalyst (Liu et al., 2017; Cao et al., 2020a, 2020b). Thus, the incorporation of g-CN into microgels is a new approach to the construction of high-performance 3D hierarchical photocatalysts. For instance (Jiang et al., 2016b), prepared a PANI/g–CN–nanosheets composite microgel with a robust and stable 3D-hierarchical structure (Fig. 6 a). This microgel showed an excellent efficiency in the removal of various organic dyes, such as methylene blue, rhodamine B, and methyl orange, due to the cooperation of adsorptive preconcentration and subsequent photocatalytic oxidation. Moreover, it has been found that g–C3N4–embedded microgels exhibit high activity in water splitting and in CO2 reduction and fixation (Sun et al., 2017). The improved photocatalytic efficiency of g–CN–based microgels was ascribed to the significant water affinity of the microgel matrix structure, which resulted in intimate contact and adsorption of molecules on the surface-active sites (Liu et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2017). Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of the microgels enables optimization by modulation of the particle size, g-CN content, and swelling behavior (Cao et al., 2020a). Importantly, g-CNs have exhibited sufficient stability and recyclability for organic pollutant photodegradation, which is of considerable significance for their practical applications.

Moreover, CM-microgels can be considered as advanced photocatalysts for the development of facile and ecofriendly strategies for H2 evolution and CO2 reduction (Lei et al., 2020). The g-CN nanosheets are anchored on the microgel matrix via molecular interactions, and their 3D intermixed architectures help to boost light absorption and prevent charge recombination, thereby improving the photocatalytic H2 evolution (Cao et al., 2020a). Meanwhile, CO2 photoreduction by microgels can be a relatively complex reaction involving the transfer of electrons at the solid-liquid interface and the generation of by-products such as CO, HCOOH, HCHO, CH3OH, CH4, and other hydrocarbons (Hu et al., 2019). It is expected that g–CN–embedded microgels will provide excellent sensitizers and good subsequent transfer, thereby promoting the photocatalytic reduction. However, up to now, there has been no scientific report focusing on the CO2 photoreduction of g–CN– and CM-based microgels.

Solar-steam generation is considered as one of the most promising technologies for mitigating the shortage of clean water by using sustainable solar energy for the desalination process (Ni et al., 2016; Tao et al., 2018). An excellent material for the solar steam generation device must have the properties of high heat insulation and good light absorption. Recently, CNT-based microgels have been applied to achieve efficient solar steam generation (Zhan et al., 2020). In this microgel system, the hollow carbon nanofibers in the CNT network structure play the role of resisting thermal transport, while the lightweight microgel can float on the water surface. The CNT-based microgel shows an excellent solar absorption (96% in the wavelength range of 250–2000 nm), and the solar steam generation device based on this CTN-microgel presents a high performance in seawater desalination, with a solar thermal conversion efficiency of 82.8%. In another study, CNTs were used as coating layers on top of PAAm microgels to enhance the solar absorption ability and increase the evaporation action and continuous water transport (Fig. 6b). The results indicated that, due to presence of the CNT layer, the PAAm-CNT microgel could maximize light absorption (>94%) by minimizing the reflection and transmission of incident light. Furthermore, the CNTs functioned as carbon heat collectors on top of the microgel, thereby inducing an energy conversion efficiency of up to 85.7% under one sun illumination.

Microgels based on rGO nanosheets have also shown unique merits for solar-steam generation, such as flexibility, light weight, and superior evaporation rate. A composite microgel was recently developed for solar-steam generation by the combination of rGO nanosheets, cellulose fibers, and sodium alginate (Storer et al., 2020). The cellulose fibers integrated with rGO considerably improved the flexibility and mechanical stability of the microgel, which exhibited a strong broad-band light absorption (96–97%) and a high evaporation rate (2.25 kg/m2 h) under 1.0 sun irradiation due to the enhanced and effective light-energy harvesting and the decreased convection energy loss. Similarly (Zhou et al., 2018), prepared an rGO-PVA microgel with a high vapor-generation rate (∼2.5 kg/m2 h) under one sun irradiation (Fig. 6c). This was attributed to the internal capillary channels in the rGO-PVA matrix, which sustained a continuous and adequate water supply for the solar vapor generation process. While PVA reduces the water evaporation enthalpy, rGO penetrates into the microgel network to provide efficient energy utilization, which can highly facilitate the water evaporation. These results demonstrated that CM-based microgels can be used as high-efficiency solar vapor generators, which opens up potential approaches and strategies for the development of environmentally-friendly and cost-effective solar desalination systems.

Water shortage, particularly in arid and semi-arid regions, is one of the most significant issues limiting crop growth. Improving soil water retention capacity and protecting against soil erosion are potential approaches to retain and supply water efficiently to plants (Deng et al., 2006). Therefore, developing effective anti-erosion and water-saving formulations is emerging as a critical approach to preserve/improve moisture retention in the soil/ground, reduce irrigation water consumption, and mitigate water shortages. Microgels are potential candidates for achieving these targets in agriculture owing to their water-adsorbing capacity and elastic networks (Jiang et al., 2018; Zaharia et al., 2018). For instance (Panova et al., 2021), used microgels for the anti-erosion stabilization of soils and grounds. They could deposit slightly viscous microgels uniformly over the earth; modifying a linear cationic polymer enhanced the strength of polymer-soil crusts, and the microgel swelling ability could improve moisture retention. Owing to such synergic features, the formulations based on the prepared microgels exhibited high efficiency in suppressing soil erosion processes and creating sufficient moisture conditions for plant development. In another study (Zheng et al., 2019), indicated that sunlight-responsive microgels based on nanodiamonds are very promising for controlled-release water carrier materials in agriculture. Furthermore, the sunlight-triggered water release ability of the microgel ensures the reversibility and repeatability of the process.

To design and develop effective microgels for drug delivery systems, some criteria need to be satisfied (Oh et al., 2008): (i) the microgels require sufficient stability for prolonged circulation in the blood stream, and the premature release of therapeutics and adverse side effects must be reduced; (ii) novel functionality is needed for the microgel surfaces to effectively recognize the receptors on diseased cells; and (iii) high thermo-responsivity is needed for light-triggered remote-control ability. Recently, numerous carbon-based microgels have been successfully developed and prepared to achieve and enhance targeted drug delivery (Wang et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2014; Oh and Lee, 2021; Wei et al., 2021). Upon exposure to various stimuli such as temperature, pH, redox potential, a magnetic field, light, or an enzyme, carbon-based microgels change their swelling behavior and fully or partially degrade their network, facilitating the release of the encapsulated active agents.

Graphene and GO have attracted considerable attention in the design of microgels for drug delivery systems because of their excellent electrical, optical, and thermal properties (Loh et al., 2010; Pumera, 2010; Zhu et al., 2010). However, GO is considered to be a more suitable candidate than graphene for the preparation of microgels as drug carriers because of the rich polar functional groups on its surface, which are beneficial for water dispersion and functional modification (Dreyer et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2013). For instance, a hollow GO-microgel (GOM) was recently produced using a simple method based on the interaction of GO with liquid nitrogen through cavitation (Fig. 7 a) (Wei et al., 2021). In the GOM formation process, cavitation is generated by liquid nitrogen, playing a crucial role in facilitating GO self-assembly at the interface. The GOM also showed a high encapsulation efficiency for the hydrophobic anticancer drug paclitaxel. The results indicated that the GO in the GOM significantly enhanced the water dispersibility of the paclitaxel and improved its efficacy. Furthermore, paclitaxel−GOMs possess promising pH-responsive characteristics, which have potential for selective drug release in an acidic tumor microenvironment and reduced adverse side effects. Moreover, because of their high photothermal sensitivity, GO-microgels have exhibited significant effects in photodynamic cancer therapy and wound healing stimulation (Wang et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2014; Oh and Lee, 2021). They basically act as photosensitive material for remote control in drug delivery systems. For example (Wang et al., 2013), successfully developed a thermosensitive microgel as an excellent carrier for the remotely controlled release of doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) by exploiting the good photothermal sensitivity of rGO as a stimulus. The rGO microgel was synthesized by the interaction of rGO that had been chemically modified by chitosan, pNIPAM, and PEG diacrylate. The rGO microgel showed a high DOX loading capacity (48 wt%), and an NIR-induced thermal effect similar to that of rGO, which showed potential for the development of on-demand drug release systems using NIR light. Similarly (Lu et al., 2014), successfully fabricated a photo- and thermo-responsive microgel composed of rGO and pNIPAM using a facile solution mixing method. This microgel presented tunable photo- and thermo-dependent transition behaviors through the control of the rGO content. These results demonstrated that GO or rGO microgels hold promise for use in drug delivery systems as anticancer drug delivery platforms because of their continuous drug release and photothermal properties, and improved therapeutic efficiency.

Creating novel and efficient solutions to improve agricultural production has recently emerged as an urgent priority to address the food requirements of the growing human population. Nutrients are often washed away from plant surfaces and are rapidly degraded or converted into compounds unsuitable for plant nutrition. Hence, developing novel fertilizer systems that can effectively bind and release nutrients to plant surfaces in a controlled manner will induce new prospects for improving plant health and increasing crop productivity. Regarding this aspect, microgels can be effectively used in foliar applications of micronutrients to ensure a sufficient nutrient supply through foliar uptake, especially of trace elements (Fe, Zn, Mn, Cu, and Mo ions) (Fernández and Eichert, 2009). For foliar applications, these nutrients must be diffused and controlled over an extended time at a high concentration and effectively transported through the plant leaf cuticle to the symplasts (Fernández and Ebert, 2005). Current formulations often use detergents, adhesives, or humectants to improve adherence and control the nutrient supply, which can cause environmental pollution (Shannon et al., 2008). Moreover, these formulations are not capable of controlling the nutrient supply over several weeks, and nutrient degradation can occur, with an ∼80% reduction (Eichert and Goldbach, 2008). Owing to their unique structure, microgels are considered excellent candidates for applications in the controlled supply of micronutrients because they can adapt well to macromolecular systems and respond to external stimuli (Pich et al., 2006). Furthermore, microgels containing reactive groups in their structure enable the integration of various functional molecules (Peng et al., 2015). For example (Meurer et al., 2017), successfully prepared a pH-responsive, biohybrid poly(allylamine) microgel that could act as a novel fertilizer system. The microgel was developed to deliver the micronutrient Fe3+ and was deposited on iron-deficient cucumber leaves. This microgel exhibited high efficiency in Fe3+ nutrient delivery owing to two key features: (i) selective loading of metal ions; (ii) strong binding to the leaf surface because of the decoration with anchor peptides. It has been demonstrated that amphiphilic anchor peptides (20–100 amino acids) can significantly enhance microgel adhesion to the leaf surface (Noor et al., 2012). In addition to micronutrients, it is believed that the microgel delivery concept may be employed to load various active molecules, such as priming compounds, fungicides, and insecticides, to improve plant viability and productivity (Meurer et al., 2017).

Because of their ability to imitate a biological extracellular matrix, hydrogels, and especially microgels, are likely to regulate the behavior of cells through providing mechanical support and signaling cues (Lutolf et al., 2009). The development and design of microgels with an appropriate mechanical strength for many cell and tissue constructs represent one of the most important strategies in medical applications (Shin et al., 2012). reported a CNT gelatin methacrylate microgel as a biocompatible, cell-responsive platform for creating cell-laden 3D constructs. NIH-3T3 cells and human mesenchymal stem cells can easily be encapsulated, spread, and proliferated in CNT microgels. It was demonstrated that the addition of CNTs significantly increased the mechanical properties of a microgel as a result of the formation of nanofiber junctions inside the microgel. These nanofiber structures created by the CNTs in the microgel played an important role in enhancing the mechanical properties, while maintaining the beneficial bioactive properties, i.e., bioactivity, high porosity, and degradability. Moreover, the CNT microgels exhibited a promising biocompatibility with human stem cells and cell morphology modulation. Furthermore, CNT microgels with a porous structure and tunable mechanical stiffness are expected to create artificial 3D scaffolds for the encapsulation of human stem cells for osteoblastic, neural lineage, and skeletal muscular niche applications. In another study, Shin's group used gelatin methacrylate in combination with GO to prepare a tunable mechanical microgel (Shin et al., 2013). In this microgel, the gelatin methacrylate served as a biocompatible surfactant to homogeneously distribute the GO in the microgel matrix. Therefore, the obtained GO-microgel system had easily tunable physical properties by controlling the GO content. The results also indicated that the GO-microgel improved the cellular behavior and offered a facile engineering strategy for constructing complex artificial tissues with mechanical durability. In summary, the introduction of CMs in microgel structures makes it possible to tune mechanical features and electrical conductivity, which opens up a promising approach for engineering heterogeneous 3D tissue constructs (Liau et al., 2012).

To enhance the survival and bio-activities of cells in transplantation applications, microgels have been broadly used for the encapsulation and delivery of cells (Choe et al., 2018, 2019b). In general, cells incorporated in microgels may be protected from host immune responses, while cell survival is also enhanced because of the maintenance of the nutrient transport and generation of cell supporting 3D environments. Recently, microgels have been used to encapsulate mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) to improve their survival and therapeutic activities in the treatment of myocardial infarction disease through promoting myocardial infarction tissue regeneration (Majka et al., 2017; Feng et al., 2022). Carbon-based nanomaterials such as CNTs, fullerenol, and GO possess antioxidant activity because of the radical scavenging efficiency of sp2 carbons (Hao et al., 2017; Han et al., 2018). Thus, they can effectively protect the cells from high oxidative stress conditions. Therefore, the development of carbon-based microgels for the encapsulation of MSCs to simultaneously imbue antioxidant activities and provide a protecting platform has been considered a promising strategy. For instance (Choe et al., 2019a), developed GO/alginate composite microgels with spherical shapes (235 μm in diameter) using an electro-spraying method for the encapsulation of MSCs (Fig. 7b). Such small microgels could also be injected through a conventional needle and support the cells without severe nutrient and oxygen depletion (Jiang et al., 2016a). Moreover, the results in this study indicated that increasing the GO content could enhance the radical scavenging efficiency and promote the antioxidant activities of the microgels, while minimizing cell damage. Hence, the presence of GO or several CMs in the microgel structure could significantly enhance the viability of the encapsulated cells as a result of their anti-oxidizing roles.

Energy consumption and climate change are major issues affecting humans, mainly caused by significant increases in the energy consumption of buildings and greenhouse gas emissions (Hsu et al., 2016). Smart windows are among the most promising ways of improving energy-saving efficiency because they can control solar energy transmission (Tian et al., 2021). In particular, glass with adjustable transparency can improve energy usage efficiency as it can effectively shield incident light and lower room temperature (Baetens et al., 2010). Recently, hybrid microgels have been employed in designing smart windows to block most amount of sunlight as the temperature rises and significantly enhance infrared transmittance modulation. A smart window can be prepared by sealing the microgels with glasses in a sandwich structure (Rotzetter et al., 2012). For instance (Chou et al., 2017), successfully developed a smart glass comprising a thermally-sensitive GO microgel with photothermal properties that responded to solar irradiation and temperature. GO, a well-known photothermal conversion material, is integrated into a thermotropic microgel to convert the photoenergy of sunlight to thermal energy. The GO microgel-based glass can screen sunlight irradiation because as the microgel temperature rises under solar light exposure, the glass becomes opaque. Consequently, the incident intensity of solar energy and room temperature reduce. In addition, GO thermotropic microgels can absorb colored organic solvents, which offers the prospect of smart glasses with arbitrary colors.

Controlling the colloidal stability of microgels, especially thermoresponsive microgels, has remained a significant challenge hindering their practical applications (Es Sayed et al., 2019). Microgels may exist in two forms: swollen in a solvent and collapsed (shrunken), The change between these two states is known as the volume phase transition. Because of their high environmental sensitivity, changes in environmental conditions, including the temperature, pH, presence of specific ions, ionic strength, solvents, and electromagnetic radiation, can cause the reversible transition of microgels (Mackiewicz et al., 2015), which results in aggregation (Gong et al., 2017), flocculation, and coagulation (Daly and Saunders, 2000). These phenomena greatly decrease the stability of the microgel system. For example, most multi-responsive microgels have shown moderate-to-poor colloidal stability under a high ionic strength condition (0.15 M) in a physiological environment (Zhang et al., 2013; Bergman et al., 2018). (Farooqi et al., 2017) also demonstrated that high temperature, low pH, and high salt concentration conditions decreased a microgel's stability. Specifically, the results indicated that microgels had poor stability with pH < 2.25 and temperature >45 °C. Generally, microgel particles are in a swollen state at low temperature, which is inherently stable because of a low effective Hamaker constant (Bergström, 1997). Simultaneously (Borrmann et al., 2018), demonstrated that a decrease in temperature below the volume phase transition temperature produced aggregation and induced low colloidal stability. These results indicated that microgels exhibit temperature-switchable colloidal stability.

The electrostatic repulsion, steric barrier, and finite elastic modulus of the network are important factors that can increase the dispersion stability of microgels (Daly and Saunders, 2000). One of the most promising strategies for improving the stability of thermoresponsive microgels is the incorporation of appropriate comonomers on the periphery of the microgel matrix, which can maintain a sufficient hydrophilicity at high temperatures (Es Sayed et al., 2019). In addition, the design and development of combined soft and porous structures having a core−shell or hollow two-shell morphology with a cross-linked network made it possible to produce microgels with different properties and architectures (Clarke and Lyon, 2016; Plamper and Richtering, 2017). Such microgels could retain their colloidal stability and swelling behavior in both water and other organic solvents (Kwok et al., 2013), which make microgel particles desirable for many applications.

NDs, CNTs, CQDs, graphene, GO, and rGOs are potential CMs for the design of microgels owing to their unique features, such as high surface areas, excellent conductivity, good chemical stability, and low costs. Owing to their excellent stability, large specific surface areas, and other unique properties, CM-based microgels are innovative microgels. In addition to the conventional techniques, several advanced technologies based on microfluidic devices and electrosprays are promising approaches for synthesizing carbon-based microgels. Various types of CMs with dimensions ranging from 0 to 3D can be employed to develop microgels. Selecting appropriate CMs is of significant importance for designing microgels with high performance and cost-effectiveness. Based on the specific properties of each CM, several important points are summarized in relation to the use of different CMs for preparing microgels: (i) 0D CMs are often incorporated into microgels to fabricate fluorescent microgels; (ii) 1D CMs are used in a microgel matrix for two purposes: to improve the stiffness and to increase the conductive properties of the microgel; (iii) 2D CMs can be incorporated into microgels for biomedical applications and as initiators for the polymerization process and photocatalyst fabrication; (iv) 3D CMs can be used to design smart microgels for biological technologies and devices. CM-based microgels have potential applications in various environmental, biomedical, agriculture, and energy fields.

Vinh Van Tran: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft preparation. Eunsol Wi: Writing – original draft preparation. Seo Young Shin: Visualization. Daeho Lee: Writing – original draft preparation. Yoong Ahm Kim: Writing – original draft preparation. Byung Chol Ma: Writing – original draft preparation, Writing – review & editing. Mincheol Chang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft preparation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
